content.full_text:
PROIS

March 12, 1980


Mr. Louis William Barnett
530 East Cypress Street
Glendale, California 91205

Dear Mr. Barnett:

Thank you for your letter of February 21, 1980, to Mr. Houston
in which you request an opinion concerning campaign disclosure
requirements of the Political Reform Act. Because your letter
raises no substantial question involving an interpretation of the
Act, no formal opinion will be issued. I hope, however, the
following response will be helpful.

You have asked how to report a reduction in an outstanding
bill owed by your committee to a creditor in exchange for services
you propose to perform for the creditor. Second, you ask how to
avoid creating an in-kind contribution to your committee by per-
forming services for the creditor.

Any transfer of services or anything else of value, whether
tangible or intangible, is a "payment" (see Govt. Code §92044),
which is a "contribution" except to the extent that full and adequate consideration is received (see Govt. Code §82215). A
"contribution" includes any monetary or non-monetary payment for
which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor
that is received by or made at the behest of a controlled committee
(see 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18215(a)).

If in the case you present the performance of your services
is deemed to be consideration for the reduction of the bill owed
to the creditor, then the reduction of the bill is not a contribution by the creditor. When your committee files its campaign
statement for the relevant period it should simply reduce the outstanding balance of accrued expenses by the appropriate amount and
attached note of explanation describing the consideration for the
reduction. The reduction should not be carried over to the summary
page of the statement so as not to distort the cash on hand figures.

Louis William Barnett                    -2-                    March 12, 1980


The performance of compensated services by you for your con-
trolled committee's benefit, on the other hand, is not done in
exchange for any consideration from the committee. Yet you are
allowing the benefit of the performance of your compensated services
to redound to your controlled committee. It is, therefore, a trans-
fer of value from you to your controlled committee, and so your
committee will receive a non-monetary contribution from you in the
amount the bill is reduced in exchange for your services. This
value should be reported by your committee as a non-monetary contri-
bution received from you in the period during which the transaction
occurs.

Because once a person becomes a candidate he remains a candidate
until he permanently ceases to raise or expend funds in connection
with his candidacy (including the retirement of outstanding obliga-
tions), your non-monetary contribution will require you to file a
candidate's statement for the relevant period. You may discharge
this obligation by filing jointly in a consolidated report
(Form 490) with your committee.)

I hope this response has been helpful. If you need additional
assistance you may call me at 916/322-5662.

Sincerely,


John Giansello
Staff Services Analyst

Louis Wm. Barnett

FPPC
FEB 25 8 43 AH '80

February 21, 1980


Mr. Tom K. Houston, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Calif.   95814

Dear Mr. Houston:

The purpose of this letter is to request a formal opinion.

In 1978, I was a candidate for the state assembly.  At this point
in time, I have one outstanding bill from that campaign which is
due a printing broker.  (Note, the bill is technically owed by
my committee and is not a person obligation.)

I have discussed doing some work for said printing broker in an
effort to "work off" the obligation.

The question or problem which I have is two fold: (1) how would I
report such a reduction in my campaign statement and (2) how do
I avoid creating an "in-kind" contribution to my own campaign
account if I obtain work from political candidates for the
printing broker?

Thanking you in advance for your help and attention, I am


Sincerely yours,

Louis Wm. Barnett
530 East Cypress Street
Glendale, Calif.   91205

sections.question: The question or problem which I have is two fold: (1) how would I report such a reduction in my campaign statement and (2) how do I avoid creating an "in-kind" contribution to my own campaign account if I obtain work from political candidates for the printing broker?
sections.question_synthetic: A former 1978 assembly candidate seeks guidance on how to report payment of an outstanding campaign debt through work performed for a printing broker, and how to avoid creating an in-kind contribution to their own campaign.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a private citizen to the FPPC, not an FPPC response. No conclusion/answer section is present. The document contains a clear question section with two specific inquiries about campaign finance reporting and in-kind contributions.

embedding.summary: A former state assembly candidate requests a formal FPPC opinion regarding reporting requirements and in-kind contribution issues related to working off an outstanding campaign debt owed to a printing broker.
